messages.content: Translate: “Đêm nó nằm trên sân thượng ngửi lại mùi hoa ngọc lan nhớ lại cái đêm hai đứa thậm chí không mặc gì mà vẫn không có xây xát gì khi đó tôi mới khóc. Đó là lần thứ hai tôi khóc trong cuộc đời. Lần thứ nhất khi giải phóng Sài Gòn, ra khỏi cánh rừng, đêm đầu tiên nằm trên giường cứng quá không ngủ được mới nằm trên võng nhớ lại những đồng đội không bao giờ trở về, nước mắt tôi chảy ra. Và lần thứ hai dù giọng và hành động rất cứng trước khi chia tay người yêu nhưng khi trở về hóa ra mình mất hết.”

"At night, lying on the rooftop, he took in the scent of the ylang-ylang flowers, remembering the night they both were even without clothes yet there was no friction, that was when I first cried. It was the second time I cried in my life. The first time was when Saigon was liberated, coming out of the jungle, the first night unable to sleep on the too hard bed, then lying in the hammock remembering comrades who would never return, my tears flowed. And the second time, despite my voice and actions being very stiff before parting with my lover, when I returned, it turned out I had lost everything."

Translate: “Một năm sau khi đang đi trên đường, có tiếng gọi tôi quay lại thấy em trai của người yêu cũ. Hai anh em uống café ở đầu Bát Đàn, tôi hỏi: “Chị khỏe không?”, cậu em sững lại: “Ơ, anh không biết gì à? Chị em mất rồi!”. Tôi hỏi tiếp: “Chị em sao mất?”. “Chị em bị ung thư” - cậu em trả lời. Lúc này tôi khớp lại mới thấy khi cô ấy gọi điện cho tôi nói muốn đi tìm lại những kỷ niệm là trước khi mất 3 tháng. Sau đó, tôi có nhờ con gái cô ấy đưa xuống mộ ở Hà Đông thắp hương và trên đường về buồn khôn nguôi chợt nghĩ, hóa ra người con gái trong chiến tranh tưởng như phản bội đi lấy chồng vì bị mất liên lạc nhưng trong sâu thẳm trong trái tim vẫn chỉ có mối tình đầu sâu đậm.”

"One year later, while I was walking down the street, I heard someone calling me. Turning around, I saw the younger brother of my ex-girlfriend. The two of us had coffee at the beginning of Bat Dan Street, and I asked, 'How is she doing?' The brother was stunned: 'Oh, you don't know? She passed away!' I continued, 'How did she pass away?' 'She had cancer,' the brother replied. At that moment, I realized that when she called me wanting to revisit our memories, it was three months before her death. After that, I asked her daughter to visit her grave in Ha Dong to light incense, and on the way back, overwhelmed with sadness, it suddenly hit me: the woman I thought had betrayed me to marry someone else during the war due to our lost contact actually held only our deep first love in the depths of her heart."
Revise the writing. Rewrite

A year had passed when, as I walked down the street, I was halted by a voice. Turning, I found myself facing the younger brother of my former lover. We sat down for coffee at the entrance to Bat Dan Street, and I inquired about her wellbeing. He froze before revealing, "Oh, you haven't heard? She's gone." Surprised, I pressed, "Gone? How?" "She succumbed to cancer," he replied.

In that moment, realization washed over me. The call she made, wanting to relive our memories, came just three months before her demise. Later on, I arranged for her daughter to visit her grave in Ha Dong to offer incense. On my return journey, engulfed in sorrow, it dawned on me: the woman I believed had moved on during the war because we lost touch, actually always harbored our profound first love deep within her heart.

pick up 2 key moments and Adapt into a 500-1000-word story (basis to develop into a screenplay), you can change some details, and remember to use Visual language as much as possible, pay attention to: - structure
- story (given circumstances, dramatic action, pace, arc)
- character (status, function, relationships, objectives and obstacles, arc)
- soulful dialogue
- idea, philosophy, atmosphere, central image. Also, put the scene the writer stood before the grave to the beggining of the story, then the departing scene between the lovers and then continue the story. The interview: "
Writer Chu Lai: I am very amorous, still am to this day, even a fleeting glimpse of a woman can haunt me. But without that, perhaps the profession of writing wouldn’t exist anymore. My first love was both a gain and a loss. We were in love, but her family did not approve, so I decided to drop out of the Military Medical University and leave the sacred theatrical stage to go to fight. She was forbidden to see me but one moonlit night, she sneaked out to visit me. We were on the rooftop of a house on The Giao Alley, known back then as Street 35 next to a park, surrounded by the intoxicating scent of ylang-ylang flowers, something miraculous happened like something out of a fairy tale, which if the younger generation hears they would laugh and say: “Mr. Chu Lai with a face like that, talking about such a story, as if it’s something nobody knows about.”
This means that before going to battle tomorrow, we spent a sleepless night together, nothing happening between us even though we were both naked at times. There was something very strange about it. The battle was going to be fierce, and we knew that half of those who went might not return. What if I didn't return? I was a hot-tempered person, recklessly thinking of leaving the consequences in her belly, but wouldn’t that burden her for the rest of her life? So many questions arose, and I gritted my teeth to hold back, so that the next morning, I could head to Hang Co station, I picked up my backpack, and she saw me off, watching the train chug away as if watching a knight who had kept her safe last night - a night where anything could have happened.
And the soldier (me), watching her figure shrink at the old station, also thought that with this posture, this face, this sentiment, if I am to live and return, she would still be there for me, because she had once said: “I can’t keep you from going, but when you return, even if you’re crippled, I will take care of you for life”.
Invisibly, the image of that girl became a spiritual anchor for me to traverse the brutal forests that seemed unbearable, striving to be an honourable soldier so that, if I survived and returned, I could look her in the eyes and say: I’ve fulfilled my duty as a soldier and come back to you without shame.”

Writer Chu Lai: For 10 years as a soldier, I was fine, luckily not too fine or I would have become a hero, and heroes can’t write. But the day I returned, I heard she was married. My sibling immediately said: “You stay here, I’ll call her over.” Two hours later, she came, still as beautiful, only now behind her Mifa bike was a wicker chair with a sleeping child. The first thing she did was cry and ask me: “Why didn’t I receive any news from you for 10 years? All the information said that you had fallen, and your family assumed you had died too. You know how many came seeking me while you were away.”
I said, “I know. The elders say leaving behind a beautiful wife or lover is like leaving a Peugeot bike in the middle of Hoan Kiem Lake without locking it. It doesn’t matter, this nation has lost too much, my personal loss means nothing.” At that point, she said: “My love for you remains unchanged. Since liberation, I have waited for you every day and asked about you. Now that you’re back, if needed, I will leave everything behind for you.” I firmly said: “It’s too late, the die is cast. We fought for the happiness of the people, including yours. You are settled now, I accept the loss.” Hearing this, she cried and… left.
On that night, I lay on the rooftop, inhaling the familiar scent of ylang-ylang blossoms. Memories of that night when we were both naked yet there was nothing happened flooded back. And then, I cried. It was the second time I had cried in my life. The first time was when Saigon was liberated. I came out of the forest and lay on a hard bed. Unable to sleep, I swung on a hammock, reminiscing about my comrades who would never return, and my tears flowed. And the second time, despite my stern voice and actions when parting ways with my lover, I realized upon returning that I had lost everything.
It was just a memory, but it also made me reflect on the heart of a woman, and the qualities of love forged by war and harsh circumstances. Yet, deep within them, there still remained something very feminine, something of a woman. For, twenty years after that meeting, a military phone with the prefix 069 rang out in the sweltering midday heat: "Hello, excuse me, are you the writer Chu Lai?" I replied: "Yes, who is this?" The woman said: "Don't you recognize me?" I said: "No, how could I when I get so many calls in a day?" When the girl said her name, I recognized her and asked again: "Why are you calling me? I thought you went abroad." At this point, she said: "No, I'm in Hanoi, just two blocks from your house. Do you have time to come here for a bit?"
Intrigued, I went to her old house and we had a cup of traditional tea. She was still as beautiful as ever, the beauty of a sophisticated middle-aged woman, but slightly haggard. After finishing our tea, she said: "Last night I dreamt of you and this morning I had to call you." In my mind, I thought to myself: "Strange, I spent my entire youth fighting in the war, while at home she married someone else, betrayed me, and then remained silent for 20 years. Living in the lofty status of a PhD, and at the brink of her midlife crisis dreams of me.”." I also said: "I think of you from time to time too, but what's up?" She said: "It's autumn now, and I want you to take me to all the places where we have memories together." I was taken aback and wondered: "Here she goes again, stirring up old memories that were buried deep down when she went off to marry someone else. And now..." I replied: "Okay, I'll make arrangements. I also want to revisit those memories but haven't had the chance yet." I promised to make arrangements, but then I forgot about it.
A year later, while I was walking down the street, I heard someone calling me; turning around, I saw the younger brother of my former lover. The two of us had coffee at the entrance of Bat Dan Street, and I asked: “How is she doing?” He froze: “Oh, you don’t know? She passed away!” I continued: “How did she pass away?” “She died of cancer,” he replied. At that moment, it dawned on me that when she called me wanting to revisit our memories, it was three months before her death. After that, I asked her daughter to take me to her grave in Ha Dong to offer incense. On the way back, overwhelmed with sadness, it struck me that the girl from the war, whom I thought had betrayed me by marrying someone else due to our lost contact, actually always harboured our profound first love deep within her heart."

In the fading light of a hazy afternoon, a solitary figure stands before a modest grave in Ha Dong. It's Chu Lai, a man whose eyes carry the weight of unspoken stories, his shoulders slightly hunched as if bearing invisible burdens. The gentle waft of incense, lit moments ago, swirls around him, an ethereal connection between the past and present. He's silent, lost in memories that are as vivid as the present moment.

Flashing back to years ago, we see Chu Lai, vibrant and full of tumultuous energy, parting from his love under a canopy of stars. The air is thick with the scent of ylang-ylang, intoxicating and heavy with unspoken promises. They had spent the night together on the rooftop, daring in their vulnerability, yet restrained by a looming future that threatened to tear them apart. "Even if you return crippled," she had whispered, "I will take care of you for life." The echo of her voice became his anchor, a vow that sustained him through the harrowing battles that awaited.

The narrative shifts to a post-war reality, a rendezvous tinged with the sorrow of what could have been. Chu Lai, returned and cloaked in the disillusionment of survival, learns of his beloved's marriage. The encounter is achingly poignant; they are two souls irreparably altered by time and circumstance. She arrives with a child in tow, embodying a life that went on in his absence. Despite the years, the bond they share is palpable, a connection that transcends even the deepest betrayals. She confesses her unwavering love, a love that waited against hope, but Chu Lai, marked by war and loss, can only see the impossibility of rekindling what was lost. They part, leaving words unsaid and hearts unsettled.

The sorrow of that night drives Chu Lai to the rooftop once more, where memories flood back with such intensity that he finds himself weeping for the second time in his life. The first had been in the aftermath of war, for comrades never to return, and now for a love irrevocably lost. His tears are a testament to the profound impacts of war, not just on land and politics but on the very fabric of human connections.

Time progresses, weaving Chu Lai's narrative into one of reflection and unresolved longing. A surprise call rekindles embers thought long dead, bringing the past sharply into focus. It's her, reaching out as if no time has passed, desiring to revisit the places that once cradled their love. Yet fate, ever unpredictable, intervenes once more, snatching away the opportunity for closure with her untimely death.

Standing before her grave, Chu Lai is a figure of contemplation. The revelation from her brother, the stark reality of her absence, forces him to confront feelings long buried. The woman he had accused, even resented, for moving on, had carried their love within her heart through it all. This realization, coming too late, fills him with an indescribable blend of grief and gratitude. As he walks away, the sinking sun casts long shadows, mirroring the bittersweet acknowledgment of a love that, despite everything, remained unblemished at its core.

In this story, the transformation of Chu Lai, from a young soldier brimming with resolve to a man haunted by the ghosts of what might have been, reflects the tragic beauty of love tested by the fires of war and time. It's a narrative that resonates with the universal truths of sacrifice, the enduring strength of love, and the poignant realization that sometimes, only in loss do we truly comprehend the depth of what we had.

Translate into English: "Em yêu thương!

4 năm trường xa cách, song ở em, mặc dù vậy, vẫn nuôi 1 sự tin tưởng tuyệt đối, luôn nhen lên trong lòng mình một ngọn lửa tình yêu nồng đượm để sưởi ấm trái tim thơ trong chờ đợi xa xôi đối với anh.

Nhiều lúc anh rất khổ tâm và oán trách cho tình yêu 2 đứa mình sao éo le vậy nhỉ? 1 năm, 2 năm, rồi 3 năm, lại gần 4 năm rồi còn gì, không một giờ gặp mặt.

Mỗi lần Xuân tới, anh lại giật mình nghĩ tới một nỗi khủng khiếp về sự mất mát của tuổi đời trôi đi mà bao ước vọng thì chỉ thấy xa vời vợi. Riêng anh, anh có thể chịu đựng được tất cả (…) bởi vì anh có tình yêu của em động viên thúc giục tháng ngày.

Còn em? Ôi, thật là một nỗi lo âu to lớn trong lòng anh. Chính vì yêu em, thương em, mà anh không thể thờ ơ nghĩ tới 1 điều xa hơn nữa cho em. Đó là tuổi đời của người con gái.

Em yêu ơi, chính vì lẽ đó mà anh trằn trọc bao đêm không ngủ. Anh nghĩ nhiều về em và dường như chỉ nghĩ về tương lai cho em tất cả, anh không mong có 1 sự ích kỷ và cũng chẳng bao giờ muốn xa em. Song anh phải nói 1 lần nữa mới đủ, vì rất yêu em mà anh phải lo tới điều đó.

…

Anh vẫn tự hỏi không biết Ánh sẽ hiểu anh như thế nào? Có thực thấu nỗi lòng thương quý của anh không hay còn nghĩ về anh thế này thế khác. Thú thực với em, trái tim anh vẫn thắm nét hình ảnh của em duy nhất. Sống ở nơi chiến trường khói lửa trong những phút quyết liệt nhất, anh vẫn thầm gọi tên em. Cái tên hết sức gợi cảm mà anh vẫn tự hào là chỉ mình anh có… Ánh vẫn là cái tên đem lại cho anh niềm vui duy nhất.

Thú thực anh không thể ràng buộc em trong một hoàn cảnh xa cách mãi như thế này. Em phải tìm lấy một hạnh phúc xứng đáng với em hơn, vì tất nhiên tuổi đời không cho phép em chờ anh mãi được. (...) Bởi vậy, anh khuyên em hãy đừng vì anh mà lãng phí tuổi xuân, hãy tìm hạnh phúc tương lai trong hiện tại để cho anh được tự hào là người đã nâng bước em đi trong ước vọng của riêng mình.

Hôm nay một ngày đầu của năm mới, Xuân 72 lại tới rồi, mỗi một mùa xuân đến mang theo bao hy vọng cho mọi người. Riêng anh, nó còn mang sự hy vọng to lớn của anh đối với em nữa. Ngày gặp em chưa thể nào biết được, mong ước rằng chúng mình sẽ được gặp nhau, trong một ngày không xa tại quê hương yêu dấu.

Em mãi mãi vẫn là hình ảnh đẹp nhất trong ký ức của anh.

Anh"."

My Beloved!

Four years of separation, yet within you, I've nurtured an unshakeable trust, always kindling a deep flame of love to warm the innocent heart that waits from afar for me.

There are times I agonize and lament over how twisted our love appears. One year, two years, then three, and nearly four years without a single meeting.

Every Spring's arrival startles me into thinking about the terrible loss of passing years, with all aspirations seeming so distant. As for me, I can endure it all (...) because I have your love to encourage and sustain me through the days.

But what about you? Oh, it's a significant worry in my heart. Because of my love and care for you, I can't help but think further for your sake. That involves the years of a woman's life.

My love, it's precisely for that reason that I've spent countless nights restless. I think so much about you and seem to only think about your future, not wanting to be selfish and never wishing to be apart from you. But I must say it one more time to make it enough, because of my deep love for you, I worry about this.

…

I still wonder how Anh will understand me? Whether she truly grasps the depth of my care or has various thoughts about me. To be honest with you, my heart still vividly bears your image alone. Living in a war-torn battlefield during the most critical moments, I silently call out your name. A name so evocative that I'm proud to say is mine alone to have… Anh remains the name that brings me unique joy.

I must admit I can't keep you bound in a situation of perpetual separation like this. You need to find a happiness that's more deserving of you, for obviously, time does not permit you to wait for me forever. (...) Therefore, I advise you not to waste your youth on me, but to seek future happiness in the present, making me proud to have uplifted you towards your own aspirations.

Today, the first day of the New Year, Spring '72 has arrived, bringing hope for everyone with each spring. For me, it also brings a grand hope for you. The day we'll meet remains unknown; I hope it will be soon, in our beloved homeland.

You will forever remain the most beautiful image in my memories.

Yours.

How about this version, how about not using long descriptions of emotions but simpler words but powerful to evoke raw emotions and sound natural more: "My Dearest Tam,

Today, a man stood at my doorstep, his eyes filled with a hope that echoed the whispers of our village. They say he’s well-mannered, a catch for any woman whose heart isn’t lost in the shadows of war.

I turned him away, Tam.

It’s been seven grueling years since you marched into a horizon painted with promises of heroism and sacrifice. And in your absence, a turmoil festers within me—a relentless storm of anger and loneliness, swirling so furiously it threatens to drown me. I clutch at anger because it’s easier than admitting how profoundly your silence pierces me.

I oscillate, Tam, between fury for your unvoiced assurances and an aching void that yearns just to know you breathe under the same sky. Maybe it’s unfair to resent you for the silence; the war likely leaves little room for words of comfort. Perhaps, just perhaps, you’ll return with tales of survival, your fingers interlocking mine once again.

The silence you’ve left me with wages its own war—a battle between holding onto the sliver of hope you’re still out there and succumbing to the dreadful possibility that I might forever linger in this limbo of not knowing. This perhaps is why I could not accept another’s proposal; there’s a fragment of me, undaunted, that preserves your space beside me, fueled by memories drenched in moonlight among the ylang-ylang trees.

Remember that night, Tam? With your bracelet now cold against my skin, a tangible relic of a dream we once lived—so vivid, yet so distant. The scent of ylang-ylang seems like a cruel memory now, a harbinger of both love and unbearable solitude. In these moments of weakness, I find myself missing you with an intensity that frightens me. It’s this silent beacon of hope that scatters any suitors who dare approach, even though prudence screams at me to let go, to move forward.

Is it folly, Tam, to wait in the shadows of an undefined future? Each day stretches into endless waiting, with only the echo of your promises for company. It’s this silence, so deafening, so all-encompassing, that carves out the hollows inside me.

So I beseech you, my love, return to me. Return safe. Return so that I might once again find wholeness in your embrace. If love alone could traverse the distances, overcome the battles, and bring you back, know that mine calls out to you, unyielding, across the expanse.

Yours, until the stars fade from the sky,

Ý"

This revised version effectively strips away the elaborate expressions of emotion found in the earlier draft for a more direct, yet equally poignant, address. The use of simpler language manages to evoke a rawer, more immediate sense of the emotional landscape Ý navigates. The contrast between the unsentimental recounting of actions—such as turning away a suitor—and the deep well of unresolved feelings for Tam provides a striking glimpse into her internal conflict. 

Without detailed descriptions, the text leans on the power of vivid, singular images—like the cold bracelet, the scent of ylang-ylang trees, and the echo of whispered promises—to evoke a sense of longing and loss. This approach renders Ý's voice more authentic, her predicament more relatable, and her resolve more admirable. 

By focusing on tangible symbols and moments (the bracelet, the trees, the doorstep encounter), the narrative invites readers to fill in the emotional depth with their personal experiences and empathies, making the story more interactive in its consumption. The statement "Yours, until the stars fade from the sky" caps the letter with dramatic simplicity, leaving a lasting impression of a love that, though questioned, persists with determined hope. This version balances stark reality with the beauty of an unwavering emotional commitment, crafting a powerful narrative that resonates deeply.
"I miss you so much that days"

It seems like there might be a small error in your sentence. If you were trying to express ongoing feelings of missing someone, a correct version could be:
"I miss you so much these days."

Or, if you meant to express feelings of missing someone in the past, you could say:
"I missed you so much in those days."
Finish the sentence: ""I miss you so much that days...
"I miss you so much that days seem to drag on endlessly, each moment stretching further away from the last time we were together."
Rewrite. shorter but more heartbroken and just
"I miss you so much that days feel like an eternity, heartache growing with each passing moment."
rewrite, more heartbroken and shattered
"I miss you so much that each day shatters me anew, leaving my heart in endless pieces."
More heartbroken, more more more heartbroken
"I miss you so much, my heart bleeds with every second, irreparably broken, drowning in despair."
regenerate
"My heart is a wasteland of longing; each moment without you fractures it more, leaving me shattered beyond repair."

Wrap up the short story for me: "UNSENT LETTER

- Hanoi, Spring 1995 -
The golden hues of dawn bathe the tombstones in a soft light. Tâm, a man in his late fifties with a face etched by time and memories, stands solemnly before a grave. His hands gently place incense in front of the headstone, the smoke curling upwards, mingling with the morning mist. His eyes, though worn, hold a depth of unshed tears. He whispers, a sorrowful tone lacing his voice, “So you were saying goodbye…”

- Hanoi, Spring 1965 -
The moon casts a soft glow over the rooftop where young Tâm and his love, Ý, both in their early twenties, bask in the intimacy of the moment, surrounded by the enchanting fragrance of ylang-ylang. Despite the warmth between them, a palpable tension hangs in the air. Tomorrow, Tâm leaves for war.
Ý spoke softly, “Even if you return crippled, I will take care of you for life.”
Tâm gazed into her eyes. They shared a tender yet charged silence, knowing well the uncertainties that lie ahead.

- Hanoi, Spring 1995 -
Tâm lost in his thoughts, barely notices the rising sun. He takes one last look at the grave, the pain of a lifetime etched into his farewell glance, and turns to leave. 
He rode his bike to their familiar park, where he and Ý once stole many kisses under the moonlit nights. Seated under a clear blue sky, he reached into his coat’s pocket and pulled out a folded yellowed letter:
“My Dearest Tâm,
A man came to my door today. He's kind, someone others would dream of. But I turned him away.
Seven whole years. You left with promises, and I haven't heard a word. 
Tâm, the loneliness is unbearable. I swing between fury and emptiness. Maybe the war steals your words. Maybe you'll return with tales of survival, your hand finding mine again.

Are you missing me?
I still keep your bracelet. Remember that night under the ylang-ylang trees? It feels like a distant dream now – sweet and faraway. But even if I’m angry, even if I’m doubting, a small part of me still holds on to belief.
Maybe that’s why I chase away the nice man. 
Is that wrong? I just want to know you’re okay. That you’re coming home. But all I have is silence and this awful, hollow feeling inside.
So please, Tâm, come back soon. Come back safe. Come back to me.
Eternally yours,
Ý.”

Tâm folds the letter carefully, as if by doing so he could preserve not just the paper but the spirit of the love encapsulated within. His heart, a relic of a bygone era, throbs with an ache that spans decades. With a deep, tremulous breath, he whispers to the empty space beside him, "Ý, my love, I did come back. But not soon enough." 

Years of silence had built an insurmountable wall between them, with Tâm’s return finding Ý no longer there to welcome him. The realization that he had been too late, that the words unspoken and letters unsent had cost them a lifetime together, filled him with a profound sorrow. Yet, holding the letter close, he felt a bittersweet connection to her, a tender yet piercing reminder of what they had—and lost.

The park around him stirred to life, the hustle of the morning brushing past him like an indifferent tide. Families laughed, children played, and lovers shared whispers beneath the trees, blissfully unaware of the man who sat among them, cradling a letter—and a love—lost to time.

As he rose to leave, the sun climbed higher, casting a warm glow that seemed to diffuse some of the shadows in his heart. Perhaps, Tâm mused as he walked away, in another life, in another time, they would find each other again. Until then, he would carry Ý with him, in the letter that he’d keep close to his heart, in the memories that even time couldn’t erase, and in the whisper of the ylang-ylang that seemed to follow him, a ghostly perfume on the breeze.

“Goodbye, my love,” he murmured, a promise and a lament, as he disappeared into the throng of the city, the unsent letter a testament to a love that, though unfulfilled, would never truly die.
